sources.notes: Statement and constraints: n <= 1e9, add any positive number consisting only of digit 9, minimize operations until the decimal representation contains digit 7.
Official editorial page for Codeforces Round 1004; checked as the contest tutorial source, but the plain fetched page only exposed lazy-loaded tutorial placeholders.
Luogu solution used to confirm the key transformation: an operation adds 10^x - 1, so for a fixed operation count one can subtract that count first and then reason digitwise.
Re-derived the fixed-k criterion by writing a sum of k operations as sum 10^{len_i} - k and distributing at most k unit increments among decimal positions.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution listing; the consulted usable solution text is recorded as the Luogu article source.

annotation.summary: Given n, repeatedly add numbers of the form 9, 99, 999, ...; find the minimum number of operations needed so that the resulting decimal representation contains digit 7.
annotation.constraints: t <= 1e4 and n <= 1e9. The answer is small: adding 9 repeatedly can force a digit 7 within at most 9 operations, so bounded enumeration is enough.
annotation.core_idea: One operation of length len adds 10^len - 1. If exactly k operations are used, their total effect is -k plus k choices of adding one unit to arbitrary decimal positions. Thus fix k from 0 to 9 and inspect m=n-k. If some decimal digit of m, including a leading zero position above the current most significant digit, can be changed to 7 by adding c unit increments at that position with 0 <= c <= k, then k operations are feasible: spend c operations with that length and put all remaining unit increments at higher positions so they do not disturb the chosen digit. Checking digits only needs modular arithmetic modulo 10; for a digit d the needed c is (7-d) mod 10. The first feasible k is optimal.
tricks: fix the number of operations and subtract it before digit reasoning
include a leading zero digit so cases like 96 + 7*99 = 789 are covered

solution_variants.name: bounded-k-digit-check
solution_variants.summary: For k from 0 to 9, test whether n-k has any digit, plus one leading zero, whose value can become 7 after at most k increments modulo 10. The first feasible k is the answer.

solution_variants.name: direct-small-search
solution_variants.summary: Equivalently, for each k enumerate the relevant operation length/digit position choices within the small bound and test whether a digit 7 appears. This is simpler to code but less explicit about the invariant.

tags.evidence: The solution depends on decimal positional digits and on interpreting 99...9 as 10^len - 1 so operations become unit increments at chosen decimal positions.
The algorithm brute-forces the operation count k from 0 to 9 and checks each decimal digit, relying on the proven small bound.
For each digit d, the number of increments needed to make it 7 is computed as (7-d) modulo 10.
For a fixed k, every sequence of k operations has the invariant form n-k plus k decimal-place unit increments.
The only coding care is scanning digits and appending one leading zero; implementation details are not the retrieval focus.
